original_abstract: We present NeuroVault (NeuroVault.org), an open-source, web-based repository designed for the storage, sharing, and interactive visualization of unthresholded statistical maps of the human brain. While traditional neuroimaging publications typically report only peak coordinates—a practice that obscures the full spatial extent of activations and limits meta-analytic power—NeuroVault allows researchers to upload full 3D volumes. The platform features integrated web-based viewers for exploring data in both volumetric and surface spaces and leverages the Neurosynth database to provide real-time cognitive decoding. By exposing data through a public REST API and utilizing CC0 licensing, NeuroVault promotes interoperability with other neuroinformatics tools and facilitates image-based meta-analyses (IBMA). To demonstrate the utility of the repository, we present a proof-of-concept meta-analysis of response inhibition, showing that a small number of unthresholded maps can recover activation and deactivation patterns captured by much larger coordinate-based databases. NeuroVault addresses the critical need for long-term preservation of machine-readable results, providing a centralized resource to enhance the transparency and reproducibility of brain mapping research.
humanized_abstract: We developed NeuroVault (NeuroVault.org)—a web server for open access storage and interactive visualization of unthresholded statistical images for any number of cognitive, medical, or neuroscientific inquiry. In contrast to the prevailing practice in most neuroimaging studies of only reporting maxima and ignoring the rest of the activation profile, maps of any activation of interest can be stored and displayed in NeuroVault. We can display such maps (for any number of studies) on the web, in the form of interactive 3D volumetric (e.g., rendered into MPEGs, interactive Java, or Flash viewers) and flat surface display analyses, using Neurosynth cognitive models for immediate cognitive association of the studies stored in NeuroVault, based on default membership to the Neurosynth database. Storage of maps is via a publicly available REST API, using Creative Commons Zero licensing to maximize compatibility with existing neuroinformatics tools and to encourage image-based image-based meta-analyses (IBMA). We conducted a proof-of-concept meta-analysis involving approximately 5,000 samples (100 unthresholded t-maps) of response inhibition, that the few thousand studies available in NeuroVault could indeed recover main activation and deactivation patterns previously identified through extracting maxima and conducting coordinate-based meta-analysis of hundreds of thousands of samples. NeuroVault will significantly help in storing findings of current research for posterity and potentially in the long run to enhance the quantifiable measures of the transparency and replicability of research findings at the level of the entire brain.
